risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used permuted block randomization at the level of the patient. Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random assignment and permuted block randomization, which are proper random methods and support a truly random allocation sequence.

Quote: "Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: The paper explicitly states that assignment was placed in a sealed envelope and opened only after eligibility, consent, and baseline assessment, which indicates a mechanism intended to prevent foreknowledge of the next allocation. Although the report does not say the envelopes were opaque and sequentially numbered, it does describe a sealed-envelope concealment procedure; in the context provided, this is the clearest explicit concealment mechanism reported.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." Comment: Although blinding was maintained only up to baseline, participants and personnel could not remain blinded once the intervention was delivered. Because the main outcomes were patient willingness, knowledge, expectations, and self-reported discussions/referrals, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence these outcomes, so overall performance bias is high.

Quote: "Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." Quote: "Participant interviews were completed at baseline and at 1, 3 & 12 months following the intervention... The telephone survey was used to capture referral information..." Comment: The key outcomes were assessed by participant self-report interviews/telephone survey, with chart review used to validate referral/orthopedic visit outcomes. Because the primary outcome (willingness) and discussion with PCP were patient-reported and participants were not blinded after the intervention, detection bias is possible for subjective outcomes; however, referral to orthopedics/seeing an orthopedic surgeon was validated by chart review and the paper states staff were blinded until after baseline. Overall, the most directly reported outcome measurement procedures indicate blinding at least during assessment initiation, but outcome assessment relied heavily on objective validation for the harder endpoints and the evidence does not show unblinded assessor influence on those measurements.

Quote: "There were no losses to follow-up except for one patient in the MI arm and one patient in DA/MI arm. Over the course of the study 93% of the subjects completed at least 2 of the 3 follow-up interviews with no differences among the 4 intervention groups (p=0.62)." Comment: Attrition was very low overall and explicitly balanced across groups, with no signal that missingness differed by intervention arm. The study also reports that most participants completed follow-up, so incomplete outcome data are unlikely to have biased the results.
